title: Tying in Universal Banks
abstract: Abstract This paper examines the tying of lending to investment banking business by universal banks. Tying may alleviate credit rationing by assuring the lender of an adequate share of the social surplus that its lending generates; however, tying raises the profitability of loans to troubled entrepreneurs, softening entrepreneurial budget constraints and reducing effort levels. When investment banking is uncompetitive, the former effect dominates, and there is too little tying; when investment banking is competitive, there is too much tying. We relate our results to the authority structure of the universal bank, which we argue is the appropriate focus for regulation.
summary_en: Should universal banks be allowed to tie loans to investment-banking services? Lóránth and Morrison build a theoretical model showing why tying matters — and when too much or too little of it occurs. Tying can alleviate credit rationing by assuring lenders of an adequate share of the social surplus from their lending. But tying also raises loan profitability for troubled entrepreneurs, softening their budget constraints and reducing effort. When investment banking is uncompetitive, the former (rationing-alleviating) effect dominates and there is *too little* tying. When investment banking is competitive, there is *too much* tying. The paper argues universal-bank authority structure is the right focus for regulation, not tying *per se*.
data_used: Theoretical paper. Game-theoretic model of universal-bank tying decisions under varying competitive structures in lending and investment banking.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian bank-supervision policy and EU CRD regulation of universal banks. The model implies tying-restriction policy should be tailored to the competitive structure of investment banking — uniform restrictions can underperform. Also implies regulatory focus should shift from tying conduct to authority structure within universal banks. Useful theoretical input for MNB and ESMA-level policy work.
